text: On entering the operating room, flexible bronchoscopy is performed.
Each segment of each lobe of each lung is examined looking for mucosal abnormalities and recurrent secretions.
A sternotomy is performed.
The pericardium is opened with electrocautery.
Throughout the procedure, the lungs are ventilated with an FiO2 of 50%, PEEP of 5 cm of water, and tidal volume of 10 mL per kg.
Ventilation is continued until the lungs are excised.
the pleural spaces are opened.
A deflation test can be performed by disconnecting the endotracheal tube to ensure complete deflation of the lungs.
The lungs are inspected by lifting them into the mediastinum, taking care to monitor the donor's hemodynamics while doing so.
Each lung is inspected for evidence of atelectasis, consolidation, and nodules.
The lungs are recruited to open up any atelectatic areas prior to performing the arterial blood gas analysis.
The Perfidex solution is hung 30 centimeters above the atrium.
Four liters of Perfidex with PGE1 is used for antegrade flush and one liter for retrograde flush divided equally between all pulmonary veins for all adults.
Silk sutures are used to retract the pericardium.
The SVC is dissected and encircled with a heavy tie or umbilical tape
A 4-0 proline per-string suture is placed in the PA at the midpoint between the pulmonary valve and pulmonary artery bifurcation.
The Perfidex lines are brought up to the table and the line is de-aired.
A silk suture is used to mark the depth of insertion of the PA cannula to ensure that it is placed in the main PA.
The PA is incised with a number 11 blade and dilated with a fine right angle.
The 24 French PA cannula is inserted and snared.
The cannula is then connected to the flush line, ensuring that there is no air in the line.
500 micrograms of prostaglandin diluted in 10 milliliters of normal saline is prepared.
When all of the teams are ready, the prostaglandin is injected directly into the main PA.
The team waits 10 to 12 heartbeats after injection.
The systolic blood pressure should drop.
The SVC is tied off.
The aorta is cross clamped.
If the heart is not being procured, this is done in the abdomen.
The IVC is transected in the pericardium in discussion with the liver team.
The left atrial appendage is cut, ensuring a 2cm orifice.
Flesh is drained into the pleural spaces to topically cool the lungs.
Once fleshed, the heart is then excised.
The cannula is removed and the PAA is divided at the midpoint between the pulmonary valve and the bifurcation at the cannulation site.
The aorta is divided.
The SVC is divided.
The left atrial division starts by entering the atrium posteriorly.
Much care is taken to ensure that adequate cuffs are left on each pulmonary vein to allow anastomosis.
This is done by dividing the atrium halfway between the pulmonary vein orifices and the coronary sinus.
It is helpful to examine the orifices from inside the atrium to ensure adequate length is being left.
Once the heart is removed, the PA flush line is attached to a large Foley catheter.
The Foley balloon is inflated with four to five cc's of air in order to include each of the pulmonary veins during the retrograde flush.
Each vein is flushed with 250 milliliters of Perfidex, ensuring that the flush is coming out of the cut pulmonary artery.
The lung explanation is then started by dividing the posterior pericardium and left inferior pulmonary ligament.
Pulling the lung into the mediastinum and right chest can aid in this dissection.
Care is taken to not injure the esophagus.
The dissection is continued superiorly, mobilizing the left posterior hilum off of the mediastinum.
Division of the posterior pericardium is completed.
The right inferior pulmonary ligament is divided and the right hilum is dissected off of the mediastinum.
Care is taken to avoid injuring the membranous airway during this dissection.
The azagus vein is divided.
Blunt dissection is used along the left lateral border of the trachea.
Blunt dissection is also used to connect this area to the anterior border of the descending aorta in the left chest.
Once connected all tissues above this dissected area are divided.
The descending thoracic aorta is transected and all remaining mediastinal attachments are divided.
As mentioned before, the lungs are ventilated until the division of the trachea.
Prior to dividing the trachea, a recruitment maneuver is performed to ensure recruitment of all areas of atelectasis.
The endotracheal tube is then pulled back to the proximal airway.
The lungs are then recruited with a sustained airway pressure of 15 to 20 centimeters of water and an FiO2 of 50%.
The trachea is then stapled when the lungs are 60 to 70% inflated, taking care to ensure that they are not overinflated.
The stapling device is reloaded and the trachea is stapled proximal to the first staple line.
The trachea is transected between the two staple lines as close to the larynx as possible.

The lungs are removed and placed in a bag filled with three liters of Perfidex solution, taking care to ensure that they do not come in direct contact with ice, but are surrounded by ice in the cooler.
